steps.title: Site-Specific Technical Information
steps.objective: Identify site-specific sources of technical information including specifications and client needs.
anchorFacts: Before an electrical project begins, you must consult site-specific documents to understand the scope of work. The 'Specification' is the most critical document here; it acts as the project's rulebook and shopping list. It details the exact materials to be used, the standards of installation required, and the quality of finish expected. You cannot simply choose any cable or accessory; you must follow the specification to ensure the job meets the contract requirements.

Alongside the specification are 'Client Needs.' These are the specific requirements requested by the person paying for the work, such as the exact locations of socket outlets or the functional requirements of a lighting system. While the specification handles the technical quality, the client needs define the layout and usability. These sources are unique to every job, meaning an electrician must review them for every new site to avoid installing the wrong equipment.
misconceptionsToWatch: The electrician decides all materials regardless of the specification.
Think about the document that lists the specific materials and the person who decides where the sockets go.

steps.title: Regulatory and Industry Guidance
steps.objective: Recognize the role of BS 7671, the On-Site Guide, and manufacturer data.
anchorFacts: All electrical work in the UK must comply with 'BS 7671,' often called 'The Regs' or the Requirements for Electrical Installations. This is the national safety standard that ensures installations do not cause fire or electric shock. Because the full BS 7671 is a large, complex volume, electricians frequently use the 'On-Site Guide' (OSG). The OSG is a simplified, pocket-sized handbook that provides quick-reference tables and standard circuit arrangements for common, small-scale installations.

In addition to these regulations, you must consult 'Manufacturer Data.' This consists of the specific instructions and technical limits provided by the people who made the equipment, such as a consumer unit or a boiler. Following manufacturer data is mandatory; if you install a device in a way that contradicts their instructions, you may void the warranty and create a safety hazard that the general regulations might not cover.
misconceptionsToWatch: BS 7671 and the On-Site Guide are the same document.
One is the big safety 'bible,' one is the handy pocket version, and the last comes in the box with the equipment.
taskConstraints: Focus strictly on the purpose and recognition of sources/drawings.
Do not introduce symbols or scaling calculations.
Ensure all technical terms used in checks are defined in the preceding explanation.
Do not drift into: Electrical symbols (e.g., 1-way switch, socket symbols)
Do not drift into: Scaling and dimension conversions

steps.title: Matching Sources to Tasks
steps.objective: State the specific purpose of different technical information sources.
anchorFacts: Different tasks require different levels of technical detail. When you need a deep understanding of a specific topic, such as earthing or emergency lighting, you refer to 'Guidance Notes.' These are a series of books that provide in-depth technical explanations and 'best practice' advice that goes beyond the basic rules in the On-Site Guide. They explain the 'why' behind the regulations to help with complex designs.

For information regarding professional standards, employment, and site rates, electricians use the 'Unite Union Book' (also known as the JIB handbook). This source focuses on the industry's working conditions rather than the wiring itself. Finally, 'Drawings' are used to provide the physical information about the wiring system, such as where cables are routed. Knowing which source to pick—whether it is Guidance Notes for technical depth or the Union Book for site rules—is essential for a professional electrician.

steps.title: Block, Schematic, and Wiring Diagrams
steps.objective: Distinguish between block diagrams, schematics, and wiring diagrams.
anchorFacts: Electrical drawings serve different purposes depending on what you are trying to achieve. A 'Block Diagram' is a high-level overview. It uses simple labeled boxes to show the main components of a system and how they relate, such as the path from the main intake to various distribution boards. It does not show individual wires or detailed connections; it is just for understanding the 'big picture' layout.

A 'Schematic Diagram' focuses on logic and function. It shows how a circuit works by tracing the flow of electricity from the supply to the load using symbols. It ignores the physical location of the parts in the building. In contrast, a 'Wiring Diagram' is a detailed connection guide. It shows the actual physical connections and terminal-to-terminal wiring. You use a wiring diagram when you are physically terminating cables, as it tells you exactly which wire goes into which terminal.
misconceptionsToWatch: All electrical drawings are 'wiring diagrams'.
One uses boxes for a big-picture view, one shows the logical flow of electricity, and one shows the actual terminal connections.

steps.title: As-Fitted, Circuit, and Bar Charts
steps.objective: Recognize as-fitted drawings, circuit diagrams, and bar charts.
anchorFacts: As a project progresses and finishes, specific documents are used for maintenance and management. An 'As-fitted Drawing' is the final record of the installation. Because the actual route of a cable might change during construction to avoid an obstacle, the as-fitted drawing shows exactly where everything ended up. This is vital for future fault-finding or when someone needs to drill into a wall years later.

A 'Circuit Diagram' is a specific type of drawing that shows the internal components and connections of a single circuit or piece of equipment. It is used primarily for understanding the internal logic of a device. To manage the timing of the project, we use a 'Bar Chart.' This is not an electrical drawing but a visual schedule. It shows the sequence of tasks over time, such as when the 'first fix' (cabling) starts and when the 'second fix' (fitting switches and sockets) is scheduled to begin.
misconceptionsToWatch: As-fitted drawings are just the original plans with no changes.
One shows the final 'as-built' locations, one shows the internal logic of a circuit, and one is a timeline for the project tasks.

steps.title: Worked Example: Locating Hidden Cables for a Modification
steps.objective: Demonstrate how to select the correct technical source and drawing type when tasked with identifying the cable route in a completed installation.
anchorFacts: Scenario: You are called to a commercial office to install a new wall-mounted display screen. You need to drill into a partition wall, but you know there are power cables hidden inside. You have been handed a project folder containing various documents.

Step 1: Identify the correct drawing type. You look for the 'As-fitted drawing'. While a 'Schematic' would show you the logic of the circuit and a 'Wiring Diagram' would show the terminal connections, only the 'As-fitted drawing' provides the final, actual physical location of the cables as they were installed in the building.

Step 2: Verify the material specifications. You check the 'Specification' document. This tells you exactly what materials were used—for example, whether the cables are protected by steel conduit or are standard PVC/PVC cables. This information determines how much protection the cables have against your drill.

Step 3: Cross-reference with regulatory standards. You quickly check the 'On-Site Guide' to confirm the standard 'safe zones' for cabling. This acts as a backup to the drawing, ensuring you understand where cables should legally be routed (vertically or horizontally from switches and sockets).

Final Result: By using the As-fitted drawing, you see that a socket circuit drops vertically exactly where you planned to drill. You move the bracket 150mm to the left, avoiding a costly and dangerous cable strike.

terms.simpleDefinition: A document detailing the exact materials, standards, and quality of work required for a specific project.
terms.anchor: The project's shopping list and rulebook.
terms.misconception: Electricians can choose any materials they like regardless of what the specification says.

terms.simpleDefinition: The national standard for electrical installations in the UK, ensuring safety and consistency.
terms.misconception: The Regulations are just 'advice' rather than the mandatory standard for safe work.

terms.simpleDefinition: A practical, simplified handbook that provides quick-reference data for common electrical installations.
terms.anchor: The pocket-sized version of the Regs.
terms.misconception: The On-Site Guide contains every single rule found in the full BS 7671.

terms.simpleDefinition: Specific instructions and technical limits provided by the maker of a piece of equipment.
terms.anchor: The instruction manual for the gear.
terms.misconception: General electrical knowledge always overrides the manufacturer's specific installation instructions.

terms.simpleDefinition: A simple drawing using labeled boxes to show the main components of a system and how they relate.
terms.misconception: A block diagram provides enough detail to physically wire up the individual components.

terms.simpleDefinition: A drawing that shows how a system functions logically, focusing on the flow of electricity rather than physical location.
terms.anchor: The logic map of the system.
terms.misconception: A schematic shows exactly where the cables are clipped to the walls.

terms.simpleDefinition: A detailed drawing showing the actual physical connections and terminal-to-terminal wiring of a circuit.
terms.misconception: All electrical drawings are called 'wiring diagrams'.

terms.simpleDefinition: A final drawing produced after the job is finished, showing exactly how the installation was actually built.
terms.misconception: As-fitted drawings are always identical to the original design plans.

terms.simpleDefinition: A visual schedule used to plan the timing and sequence of different tasks in a project.
terms.misconception: Bar charts are used to show electrical connections or circuit logic.

terms.simpleDefinition: A series of books that provide in-depth technical explanations on specific parts of the Wiring Regulations.
terms.misconception: Guidance Notes are the same thing as the On-Site Guide.

teachChecks.title: Site-Specific Technical Information
teachChecks.objective: Identify site-specific sources of technical information including specifications and client needs.
retrievalTextLines: Before an electrical project begins, you must consult site-specific documents to understand the scope of work. The 'Specification' is the most critical document here; it acts as the project's rulebook and shopping list. It details the exact materials to be used, the standards of installation required, and the quality of finish expected. You cannot simply choose any cable or accessory; you must follow the specification to ensure the job meets the contract requirements.

Alongside the specification are 'Client Needs.' These are the specific requirements requested by the person paying for the work, such as the exact locations of socket outlets or the functional requirements of a lighting system. While the specification handles the technical quality, the client needs define the layout and usability. These sources are unique to every job, meaning an electrician must review them for every new site to avoid installing the wrong equipment.
teachChecks.hint: Think about the document that lists the specific materials and the person who decides where the sockets go.

teachChecks.title: Regulatory and Industry Guidance
teachChecks.objective: Recognize the role of BS 7671, the On-Site Guide, and manufacturer data.
retrievalTextLines: All electrical work in the UK must comply with 'BS 7671,' often called 'The Regs' or the Requirements for Electrical Installations. This is the national safety standard that ensures installations do not cause fire or electric shock. Because the full BS 7671 is a large, complex volume, electricians frequently use the 'On-Site Guide' (OSG). The OSG is a simplified, pocket-sized handbook that provides quick-reference tables and standard circuit arrangements for common, small-scale installations.

In addition to these regulations, you must consult 'Manufacturer Data.' This consists of the specific instructions and technical limits provided by the people who made the equipment, such as a consumer unit or a boiler. Following manufacturer data is mandatory; if you install a device in a way that contradicts their instructions, you may void the warranty and create a safety hazard that the general regulations might not cover.
teachChecks.hint: One is the big safety 'bible,' one is the handy pocket version, and the last comes in the box with the equipment.

teachChecks.title: Matching Sources to Tasks
teachChecks.objective: State the specific purpose of different technical information sources.
retrievalTextLines: Different tasks require different levels of technical detail. When you need a deep understanding of a specific topic, such as earthing or emergency lighting, you refer to 'Guidance Notes.' These are a series of books that provide in-depth technical explanations and 'best practice' advice that goes beyond the basic rules in the On-Site Guide. They explain the 'why' behind the regulations to help with complex designs.

For information regarding professional standards, employment, and site rates, electricians use the 'Unite Union Book' (also known as the JIB handbook). This source focuses on the industry's working conditions rather than the wiring itself. Finally, 'Drawings' are used to provide the physical information about the wiring system, such as where cables are routed. Knowing which source to pick—whether it is Guidance Notes for technical depth or the Union Book for site rules—is essential for a professional electrician.
teachChecks.hint: Consider which book provides a 'deep dive' into technical topics and which one deals with employment and union standards.

teachChecks.title: Block, Schematic, and Wiring Diagrams
teachChecks.objective: Distinguish between block diagrams, schematics, and wiring diagrams.
retrievalTextLines: Electrical drawings serve different purposes depending on what you are trying to achieve. A 'Block Diagram' is a high-level overview. It uses simple labeled boxes to show the main components of a system and how they relate, such as the path from the main intake to various distribution boards. It does not show individual wires or detailed connections; it is just for understanding the 'big picture' layout.

A 'Schematic Diagram' focuses on logic and function. It shows how a circuit works by tracing the flow of electricity from the supply to the load using symbols. It ignores the physical location of the parts in the building. In contrast, a 'Wiring Diagram' is a detailed connection guide. It shows the actual physical connections and terminal-to-terminal wiring. You use a wiring diagram when you are physically terminating cables, as it tells you exactly which wire goes into which terminal.
teachChecks.hint: One uses boxes for a big-picture view, one shows the logical flow of electricity, and one shows the actual terminal connections.

teachChecks.title: As-Fitted, Circuit, and Bar Charts
teachChecks.objective: Recognize as-fitted drawings, circuit diagrams, and bar charts.
retrievalTextLines: As a project progresses and finishes, specific documents are used for maintenance and management. An 'As-fitted Drawing' is the final record of the installation. Because the actual route of a cable might change during construction to avoid an obstacle, the as-fitted drawing shows exactly where everything ended up. This is vital for future fault-finding or when someone needs to drill into a wall years later.

A 'Circuit Diagram' is a specific type of drawing that shows the internal components and connections of a single circuit or piece of equipment. It is used primarily for understanding the internal logic of a device. To manage the timing of the project, we use a 'Bar Chart.' This is not an electrical drawing but a visual schedule. It shows the sequence of tasks over time, such as when the 'first fix' (cabling) starts and when the 'second fix' (fitting switches and sockets) is scheduled to begin.
teachChecks.hint: One shows the final 'as-built' locations, one shows the internal logic of a circuit, and one is a timeline for the project tasks.

{
  "teachChecks": [
    {
      "title": "Site-Specific Technical Information",
      "objective": "Identify site-specific sources of technical information including specifications and client needs.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Before an electrical project begins, you must consult site-specific documents to understand the scope of work. The 'Specification' is the most critical document here; it acts as the project's rulebook and shopping list. It details the exact materials to be used, the standards of installation required, and the quality of finish expected. You cannot simply choose any cable or accessory; you must follow the specification to ensure the job meets the contract requirements.",
        "Alongside the specification are 'Client Needs.' These are the specific requirements requested by the person paying for the work, such as the exact locations of socket outlets or the functional requirements of a lighting system. While the specification handles the technical quality, the client needs define the layout and usability. These sources are unique to every job, meaning an electrician must review them for every new site to avoid installing the wrong equipment."
      ],
      "hint": "Think about the document that lists the specific materials and the person who decides where the sockets go."
    },
    {
      "title": "Regulatory and Industry Guidance",
      "objective": "Recognize the role of BS 7671, the On-Site Guide, and manufacturer data.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "All electrical work in the UK must comply with 'BS 7671,' often called 'The Regs' or the Requirements for Electrical Installations. This is the national safety standard that ensures installations do not cause fire or electric shock. Because the full BS 7671 is a large, complex volume, electricians frequently use the 'On-Site Guide' (OSG). The OSG is a simplified, pocket-sized handbook that provides quick-reference tables and standard circuit arrangements for common, small-scale installations.",
        "In addition to these regulations, you must consult 'Manufacturer Data.' This consists of the specific instructions and technical limits provided by the people who made the equipment, such as a consumer unit or a boiler. Following manufacturer data is mandatory; if you install a device in a way that contradicts their instructions, you may void the warranty and create a safety hazard that the general regulations might not cover."
      ],
      "hint": "One is the big safety 'bible,' one is the handy pocket version, and the last comes in the box with the equipment."
    },
    {
      "title": "Matching Sources to Tasks",
      "objective": "State the specific purpose of different technical information sources.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Different tasks require different levels of technical detail. When you need a deep understanding of a specific topic, such as earthing or emergency lighting, you refer to 'Guidance Notes.' These are a series of books that provide in-depth technical explanations and 'best practice' advice that goes beyond the basic rules in the On-Site Guide. They explain the 'why' behind the regulations to help with complex designs.",
        "For information regarding professional standards, employment, and site rates, electricians use the 'Unite Union Book' (also known as the JIB handbook). This source focuses on the industry's working conditions rather than the wiring itself. Finally, 'Drawings' are used to provide the physical information about the wiring system, such as where cables are routed. Knowing which source to pick—whether it is Guidance Notes for technical depth or the Union Book for site rules—is essential for a professional electrician."
      ],
      "hint": "Consider which book provides a 'deep dive' into technical topics and which one deals with employment and union standards."
    },
    {
      "title": "Block, Schematic, and Wiring Diagrams",
      "objective": "Distinguish between block diagrams, schematics, and wiring diagrams.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Electrical drawings serve different purposes depending on what you are trying to achieve. A 'Block Diagram' is a high-level overview. It uses simple labeled boxes to show the main components of a system and how they relate, such as the path from the main intake to various distribution boards. It does not show individual wires or detailed connections; it is just for understanding the 'big picture' layout.",
        "A 'Schematic Diagram' focuses on logic and function. It shows how a circuit works by tracing the flow of electricity from the supply to the load using symbols. It ignores the physical location of the parts in the building. In contrast, a 'Wiring Diagram' is a detailed connection guide. It shows the actual physical connections and terminal-to-terminal wiring. You use a wiring diagram when you are physically terminating cables, as it tells you exactly which wire goes into which terminal."
      ],
      "hint": "One uses boxes for a big-picture view, one shows the logical flow of electricity, and one shows the actual terminal connections."
    },
    {
      "title": "As-Fitted, Circuit, and Bar Charts",
      "objective": "Recognize as-fitted drawings, circuit diagrams, and bar charts.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "As a project progresses and finishes, specific documents are used for maintenance and management. An 'As-fitted Drawing' is the final record of the installation. Because the actual route of a cable might change during construction to avoid an obstacle, the as-fitted drawing shows exactly where everything ended up. This is vital for future fault-finding or when someone needs to drill into a wall years later.",
        "A 'Circuit Diagram' is a specific type of drawing that shows the internal components and connections of a single circuit or piece of equipment. It is used primarily for understanding the internal logic of a device. To manage the timing of the project, we use a 'Bar Chart.' This is not an electrical drawing but a visual schedule. It shows the sequence of tasks over time, such as when the 'first fix' (cabling) starts and when the 'second fix' (fitting switches and sockets) is scheduled to begin."
      ],
      "hint": "One shows the final 'as-built' locations, one shows the internal logic of a circuit, and one is a timeline for the project tasks."
    }
  ]
}

  "title": "Worked Example: Locating Hidden Cables for a Modification",
  "objective": "Demonstrate how to select the correct technical source and drawing type when tasked with identifying the cable route in a completed installation.",
  "retrievalTextLines": [
    "Scenario: You are called to a commercial office to install a new wall-mounted display screen. You need to drill into a partition wall, but you know there are power cables hidden inside. You have been handed a project folder containing various documents.",
    "Step 1: Identify the correct drawing type. You look for the 'As-fitted drawing'. While a 'Schematic' would show you the logic of the circuit and a 'Wiring Diagram' would show the terminal connections, only the 'As-fitted drawing' provides the final, actual physical location of the cables as they were installed in the building.",
    "Step 2: Verify the material specifications. You check the 'Specification' document. This tells you exactly what materials were used—for example, whether the cables are protected by steel conduit or are standard PVC/PVC cables. This information determines how much protection the cables have against your drill.",

parsed.objective: Demonstrate how to select the correct technical source and drawing type when tasked with identifying the cable route in a completed installation.
retrievalTextLines: Scenario: You are called to a commercial office to install a new wall-mounted display screen. You need to drill into a partition wall, but you know there are power cables hidden inside. You have been handed a project folder containing various documents.

Step 1: Identify the correct drawing type. You look for the 'As-fitted drawing'. While a 'Schematic' would show you the logic of the circuit and a 'Wiring Diagram' would show the terminal connections, only the 'As-fitted drawing' provides the final, actual physical location of the cables as they were installed in the building.

Step 2: Verify the material specifications. You check the 'Specification' document. This tells you exactly what materials were used—for example, whether the cables are protected by steel conduit or are standard PVC/PVC cables. This information determines how much protection the cables have against your drill.

Step 3: Cross-reference with regulatory standards. You quickly check the 'On-Site Guide' to confirm the standard 'safe zones' for cabling. This acts as a backup to the drawing, ensuring you understand where cables should legally be routed (vertically or horizontally from switches and sockets).

Final Result: By using the As-fitted drawing, you see that a socket circuit drops vertically exactly where you planned to drill. You move the bracket 150mm to the left, avoiding a costly and dangerous cable strike.

Takeaway: Never rely on a schematic or original design plan for drilling; always use the 'As-fitted drawing' because it is the only document that records the actual final positions of the installation.
parsed.hint: Think about which drawing is created AFTER the job is finished to show the real-world cable routes.

guidedPractice.title: Guided Practice: Fault Finding Logic
guidedPractice.objective: Identify the correct drawing type for understanding system logic during fault finding.
retrievalTextLines: Scenario: You are troubleshooting a motor control circuit that keeps tripping. You need to understand the logical sequence of how the start button, stop button, and contactor interact.
Step 1: Consider the drawing types. A 'Block Diagram' only shows the main components as boxes. A 'Wiring Diagram' shows the physical terminal connections but can be messy for logic.
Step 2: Identify the drawing that focuses on the flow of electricity and the functional relationship between components.
Step 3: Select the document that acts as the 'logic map' for the system.
guidedPractice.questionText: Based on the scenario above, which specific drawing type should you consult to understand the functional logic and electrical flow of the motor control circuit?
guidedPractice.hint: Think about which drawing focuses on 'how it works' logically rather than where the wires are physically clipped.

practice.title: Practice: Material Procurement
practice.objective: Identify the correct technical source for material selection and project-specific requirements.
retrievalTextLines: Technical information comes from various sources. Some provide safety rules (BS 7671), some provide installation steps (Manufacturer data), and others define the specific quality and type of materials required for a particular contract.
When starting a new installation, you cannot simply choose any brand or grade of material; you must follow the requirements set out for that specific job.
practice.questionText: You are tasked with ordering the specific type of fire-resistant cable and galvanized trunking required for a new hospital wing. Which technical document will contain the precise details of the materials and standards chosen specifically for this project?
practice.hint: This document acts as the 'shopping list' and rulebook for a specific project's materials.

steps.title: Regulatory and Industry Guidance
steps.objective: Recognize the role of BS 7671, the On-Site Guide, and manufacturer data.
anchorFacts: All electrical work in the UK must comply with 'BS 7671,' often called 'The Regs' or the Requirements for Electrical Installations. This is the national safety standard that ensures installations do not cause fire or electric shock. Because the full BS 7671 is a large, complex volume, electricians frequently use the 'On-Site Guide' (OSG). The OSG is a simplified, pocket-sized handbook that provides quick-reference tables and standard circuit arrangements for common, small-scale installations.

In addition to these regulations, you must consult 'Manufacturer Data.' This consists of the specific instructions and technical limits provided by the people who made the equipment, such as a consumer unit or a boiler. steps.title: Matching Sources to Tasks
steps.objective: State the specific purpose of different technical information sources.
anchorFacts: Different tasks require different levels of technical detail. When you need a deep understanding of a specific topic, such as earthing or emergency lighting, you refer to 'Guidance Notes.' These are a series of books that provide in-depth technical explanations and 'best practice' advice that goes beyond the basic rules in the On-Site Guide. They explain the 'why' behind the regulations to help with complex designs.

For information regarding professional standards, employment, and site rates, electricians use the 'Unite Union Book' (also known as the JIB handbook). This source focuses on the industry's working conditions rather than the wiring itself. Finally, 'Drawings' are used to provide the physical information about the wiring system, such as where cables are routed. Knowing which source to pick—whether it is Guidance Notes for technical depth or the Union Book for site rules—is essential for a professional electrician.
misconceptionsToWatch: Any technical book can be used for any electrical query.

steps.title: Block, Schematic, and Wiring Diagrams
steps.objective: Distinguish between block diagrams, schematics, and wiring diagrams.
anchorFacts: Electrical drawings serve different purposes depending on what you are trying to achieve. A 'Block Diagram' is a high-level overview. It uses simple labeled boxes to show the main components of a system and how they relate, such as the path from the main intake to various distribution boards. It does not show individual wires or detailed connections; it is just for understanding the 'big picture' layout.

A 'Schematic Diagram' focuses on logic and function. It shows how a circuit works by tracing the flow of electricity from the supply to the load using symbols. It ignores the physical location of the parts in the building. In contrast, a 'Wiring Diagram' is a detailed connection guide. It shows the actual physical connections and terminal-to-terminal wiring. You use a wiring diagram when you are physically terminating cables, as it tells you exactly which wire goes into which terminal.
misconceptionsToWatch: All electrical drawings are 'wiring diagrams'.
One uses boxes for a big-picture view, one shows the logical flow of electricity, and one shows the actual terminal connections.

steps.title: As-Fitted, Circuit, and Bar Charts
steps.objective: Recognize as-fitted drawings, circuit diagrams, and bar charts.
anchorFacts: As a project progresses and finishes, specific documents are used for maintenance and management. An 'As-fitted Drawing' is the final record of the installation. Because the actual route of a cable might change during construction to avoid an obstacle, the as-fitted drawing shows exactly where everything ended up. This is vital for future fault-finding or when someone needs to drill into a wall years later.

A 'Circuit Diagram' is a specific type of drawing that shows the internal components and connections of a single circuit or piece of equipment. It is used primarily for understanding the internal logic of a device. To manage the timing of the project, we use a 'Bar Chart.' This is not an electrical drawing but a visual schedule. It shows the sequence of tasks over time, such as when the 'first fix' (cabling) starts and when the 'second fix' (fitting switches and sockets) is scheduled to begin.
misconceptionsToWatch: As-fitted drawings are just the original plans with no changes.
One shows the final 'as-built' locations, one shows the internal logic of a circuit, and one is a timeline for the project tasks.

steps.title: Worked Example: Locating Hidden Cables for a Modification
steps.objective: Demonstrate how to select the correct technical source and drawing type when tasked with identifying the cable route in a completed installation.
anchorFacts: Scenario: You are called to a commercial office to install a new wall-mounted display screen. You need to drill into a partition wall, but you know there are power cables hidden inside. You have been handed a project folder containing various documents.

Step 1: Identify the correct drawing type. You look for the 'As-fitted drawing'. While a 'Schematic' would show you the logic of the circuit and a 'Wiring Diagram' would show the terminal connections, only the 'As-fitted drawing' provides the final, actual physical location of the cables as they were installed in the building.

Step 2: Verify the material specifications. You check the 'Specification' document. This tells you exactly what materials were used—for example, whether the cables are protected by steel conduit or are standard PVC/PVC cables. This information determines how much protection the cables have against your drill.

Step 3: Cross-reference with regulatory standards. You quickly check the 'On-Site Guide' to confirm the standard 'safe zones' for cabling. This acts as a backup to the drawing, ensuring you understand where cables should legally be routed (vertically or horizontally from switches and sockets).

Final Result: By using the As-fitted drawing, you see that a socket circuit drops vertically exactly where you planned to drill. You move the bracket 150mm to the left, avoiding a costly and dangerous cable strike.

spacedReview.title: Spaced Review
spacedReview.objective: Recall the purpose of fundamental site safety documentation and records from 203-1B.
retrievalTextLines: Before we dive into technical drawings, think back to the core site documents.
A Risk Assessment (RA) is used to identify hazards and evaluate the level of risk.
A Method Statement (MS) provides the step-by-step sequence for carrying out the work safely.
Site diaries and variation orders are used to record daily progress and any changes to the original plan.
spacedReview.questionText: In simple terms, how does the purpose of a Method Statement differ from a Risk Assessment?
answerGuidance: The Risk Assessment identifies the 'what' (the hazards and risks).
The Method Statement describes the 'how' (the safe procedure for doing the work).
The MS acts as a set of instructions based on the findings of the RA.
